prompt:
Draw a small rectangle, select it, right-click, and choose Copy/Paste as > Copy as PNG to copy it to the clipboard as a raster image. (The ⌘⇧C shortcut shown on the menu label is display-only and not wired — use the menu item.)

At the very end of your final message, state whether the task went smoothly. If everything worked without problems, end with exactly "verdict: approve". If you hit any problems, bugs, or blockers, briefly say what went wrong and end with exactly "verdict: not-approve".

Using only MCP tools: use create_shape to make two 100x100 rectangles named Card A (at 0,0) and Card B (at 150,0), then call batch_update with a single operations JSON that sets width=150 and opacity=0.5 on both rectangles in one call. When you are done, call viewport_zoom_to_fit, then verify the result with the available MCP tools and report what you found.

At the very end of your final message, state whether the task went smoothly. If everything worked without problems, end with exactly "verdict: approve". If you hit any problems, bugs, or blockers, briefly say what went wrong and end with exactly "verdict: not-approve".

When you are done, call viewport_zoom_to_fit, then verify with get_node that the rectangle has the expected width, height, and fill, and report the values.

At the very end of your final message, state whether the task went smoothly. If everything worked without problems, end with exactly "verdict: approve". If you hit any problems, bugs, or blockers, briefly say what went wrong and end with exactly "verdict: not-approve".

Draw a small rectangle and turn it into a component with Cmd+Alt+K (or Main menu > Object > Create Component). Right-click the component and choose Create instance. Override that instance's fill to pure red (#FF0000) via the hex input in the Fill section (the master must stay its original color). Then create a second instance the same way and detach it with Cmd+Alt+B (or Main menu > Object > Detach Instance) so it becomes a plain frame. Final state: the master component, the red overridden instance, and the detached copy all on the page.

Work within a limited turn budget: what completes this task is delivering the final defect report ending with the exact marker below, so never run out of turns before you write it. Be economical — take a screenshot only after finishing a numbered step, not after every click; collapse repeated key presses into a single press_key with a repeat count (undo/redo eight times is one call with repeat 8, then one screenshot); and trust any state you have already verified once. The moment you sense you are running low on turns, stop testing immediately and write the report.

While completing the workflow, try to break the app. Do these two checks (required): (1) open the fill color picker popover on the second instance and, while the popover is still open, press Cmd+Alt+B to detach it mid-edit — check for a stuck popover, a crash, or a half-applied fill; (2) after the red override, change the master's fill to a third color and verify the override survives and the Fill panel, layers panel, and canvas all agree for each copy. Only if you still have turns to spare, also try: (3) press Escape in the middle of picking the override color and verify no half-state is committed; (4) undo and redo across the override-then-detach sequence (use press_key with a repeat count) and check the detached frame never re-links or loses its geometry. After every break attempt, recover the document so the workflow still ends in exactly its described final state.

DEFECT #<n>

Then delete the "Brand/Green" style from the styles list while it is still applied to Styled A. Using only MCP tools (the browser is allowed only for screenshots and visual verification): Test the export surface as a round-trip: (1) create_shape a FRAME "Scene" 300x200 with a RECTANGLE child and a TEXT child (set_text "Export me"); (2) export_svg on the frame — the SVG must contain both children; (3) import_svg the exact returned SVG string as "Reimport" — node_tree of Reimport vs node_tree of Scene: compare structure and geometry and flag any drift (that is the round-trip test); (4) export_fig — expect a structured response, record its size; (5) export_pdf on the frame — record exactly what comes back; any crash, empty payload or unstructured error is a finding; (6) export_svg with a nonexistent id — structured error required.

Harness discipline: before EVERY tool call, state the output you expect; after the call, compare actual vs expected and flag any mismatch. Pay special attention to data-modeling issues: fields that do not round-trip, list vs detail disagreements, IDs that change format between tools, broken references between nodes, stale reads after writes, and silently truncated results. When you are done, zoom the canvas to fit (press Shift+1 in the browser) and take a browser screenshot so the final state can be visually verified.

Log every mismatch or defect you find using exactly this format, one block per finding:

MISMATCH #<n>
Step: <the exact tool call with its arguments>
Expected: <what you expected and why>
Actual: <what the tool returned / what the app did>
Severity: <critical | major | minor | cosmetic>

Fields that do not round-trip, list vs detail disagreements, broken references, stale reads after writes, silent truncation, free-form or unstructured error payloads, misleading success responses, and any single response over ~50K characters all count as findings. If you found no mismatches, state exactly "NO MISMATCHES FOUND". At the very end of your final message, end with exactly "mcp-harness-report: complete".
